agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Ghrelin -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Because ghrelin is involved in reward-processing, the ghrelin system is also being studied as a target in the context of alcohol misuse disorder. Rodent studies have found that administration of ghrelin increased alcohol intake, while ghrelin receptor blockade had the opposite effect. Preliminary studies involving heavy users of alcohol have found that ghrelin administration increases alcohol cravings, while PF-5190457, a GHS-R1-alpha-blocker, appeared to reduce cravings. [9]

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Ghrelin -- Mechanism
agent1_snippets.content: Once secreted, ghrelin exerts its orexigenic effects by binding to the ghrelin/growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHS-R1-alpha). [2] GHS-R1-alpha receptors appear on the arcuate and the ventromedial nuclei of the hypothalamus; these cells also host the receptors for leptin, a satiety hormone that opposes the effects of ghrelin. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Gastric Inhibitory Peptide -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: GIP receptors have also become therapeutic targets in the development of new antidiabetic medications. Tirzepatide, a dual GLP-1 and GIP receptor agonist, has demonstrated superior efficacy in weight reduction and glycemic control compared with GLP-1 receptor agonists such as semaglutide, particularly at higher doses of 10 and 15 mg. [47] [48] Tirzepatide has also received U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval for the treatment of obstructive sleep apnea, and ongoing clinical studies are investigating its potential benefits in several other conditions, including polycystic ovarian syndrome, psoriasis, cardiovascular disease, and alcohol use disorder. Furthermore, triple-receptor agonists such as reatrutide are in phase III clinical trials and may provide additional therapeutic benefit in weight management and T2DM.

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Ghrelin -- Function
agent1_snippets.content: Ghrelin contributes to a large number of bodily functions, including appetite regulation and fat storage, inhibition of insulin secretion, stimulation of growth hormone release, reward processing, increased gastric acid secretion, intestinal motility, and other potential effects. It circulates in 2 forms, acyl-Ghrelin, and des-acyl ghrelin. [2] The former is the most potent form and is essential for the engagement of the GHS-R1α while the latter is the predominant circulating form with no identified receptor. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Issues of Concern -- Retatrutide
agent1_snippets.content: Retatrutide (manufactured by Eli Lilly) represents the next evolution in incretin-based therapy as a triple GLP-1/GIP/glucagon receptor agonist currently in phase 3 trials. Phase 2 data demonstrated an unprecedented 24.2% weight loss at 48 weeks with the highest dose, surpassing the weight loss achieved with all currently available medications. The triple mechanism targets complementary pathways in glucose homeostasis, appetite regulation, and energy expenditure, providing synergistic effects that translate into superior clinical outcomes.

agent1_snippets.title: Non-Dieting Approaches to Treatment of Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- 1. Tirzepatide
agent1_snippets.content: Controversies surrounding the drug and trials: Some studies have shown that GIP is, in fact, obesogenic and there have been GIP antagonists developed to tackle obesity. GIP itself can contribute to hyperglycemia because of the stimulation of glucagon and can inhibit the effect of GLP-1 on decreasing food intake. Hence at least per laboratory data, using a GIP agonist is of limited utility. However, the trial investigators argue that GIP contributes more to the incretin effect than GLP-1, and GIP-induced weight loss is via glucagon's anti-lipogenic and anorexic action. Thus having a dual agonist was more effective than a single agent. Another criticism is that the dose of GLP-1 agonists used in the SURPASS trials to show superiority is suboptimal compared to the dose currently being studied to treat obesity.

agent1_snippets.title: Setmelanotide -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Setmelanotide is a type of MC4R agonist with 20-fold less activity at the melanocortin-3 receptor (MC3R) and melanocortin-1 receptor (MC1R). The MC4R can be endogenously activated by the binding of melanocyte-stimulating hormones (MSH), which are involved in responding to acute signal regulations of hunger and satiety. [8] [9] Setmelanotide acts on the MC4R pathway to reverse hyperphagia and promote weight loss through decreased caloric intake and increased energy expenditure. [2] [10]

agent1_snippets.title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Issues of Concern -- Survodutide
agent1_snippets.content: Survodutide (manufactured by Boehringer Ingelheim) is a novel dual GLP-1/glucagon receptor agonist that achieved 18.7% weight loss in phase 2 trials. Adding glucagon receptor agonism to GLP-1 activity enhances metabolic rate and energy expenditure, providing a complementary mechanism to appetite suppression, thereby achieving superior weight loss outcomes. Survodutide has demonstrated direct hepatic effects, including enhanced fat mobilization, lipolysis, increased energy expenditure, and a reduction in hepatic steatosis and fibrotic processes, in clinical trials.

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Ghrelin -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Another potential clinical application of ghrelin is in the treatment of gastroparesis, a disease characterized by delayed gastric emptying causing symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, pain, early satiety, and bloating. Ghrelin receptor agonists have been a topic of research as a treatment for gastroparesis due to ghrelin’s prokinetic effects on gastrointestinal motility. [21] Ghrelin receptor agonists may be useful in numerous other conditions involving gastrointestinal motility issues, including functional dyspepsia and postoperative ileus. [16]

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Appetite Regulation
agent1_snippets.content: Ghrelin – Classically considered the “hunger hormone” due to its appetite stimulation. It is a 28 amino-acid peptide synthesized predominantly in the stomach, specifically the gastric fundus where oxyntic gland P/D1 cells are found. [143] These cells come in 2 types. The open type is exposed to the lumen of the stomach and is secreted directly into the stomach to intermix with gastric contents. The closed type is found close to the lamina propria and is secreted directly into the vasculature. [144] Ghrelin is also present in the pancreas, placenta, kidney, and pituitary but at much lower levels. [145] Ghrelin receptors (growth hormone secretagogue receptor [GHS-R]) have 2 forms: GHS-R1a and GHS-R1b. GHS-R1a is found in the central nervous system and peripheral tissue and mediates food intake and satiety. GHS-R1b has a wider distribution, but its function is unknown since it has no links to the same G protein complex that GHS-R1a is. [146] [147] Ghrelin levels increase during fasting, starvation, and anorexia – surges are also noted before meals. Nutrients suppress ghrelin, carbohydrates having the greatest inhibitory effect, followed by protein and lipids. The decrease in ghrelin occurs via non-vagal signals from the stomach and intestines. [148]

A: yes
B: no

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Ghrelin -- Cellular Level
agent1_snippets.content: Ghrelin gets processed as preproghrelin, which is cleaved to proghrelin and finally ghrelin in the cell. [2] Proghrelin undergoes a unique post-translational modification with octanolyation of the serine at position 3, resulting in acylating ghrelin, the most active form of ghrelin. [2] Mediation of this acylation of ghrelin is via ghrelin-o-acyltransferase (GOAT) and underlies its role as an endogenous ligand of the growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHS-R1-alpha). This engagement of GHS-R1-alpha results in the secretion of growth hormone from the pituitary gland. [3] Acylated ghrelin also binds to the GHS-R1-alpha in neuronal circuits in the hypothalamus resulting in its orexigenic effects. GHS-R1α is a G protein-coupled receptor that results in the activation of phospholipase C, an increase in inositol triphosphate, and an increase in calcium release. [3] Ghrelin gets transported to the hypothalamus and other areas of the brain via the circulation and vagal afferents. [3] In the hypothalamic orexigenic axis, ghrelin triggers the release of 2 crucial neuropeptides- neuropeptide Y (NPY) and agouti-related protein (AgRP); this stimulates appetite and also reduces energy expenditure. [3] [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Ghrelin -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Ghrelin is a peptide hormone known for its role in the stimulation of appetite and feeding behavior, energy homeostasis, and carbohydrate metabolism. Ghrelin’s orexigenic (appetite-stimulating) effects get mediated via metabolic need-driven homeostatic feeding as well as central actions on reward, memory, and motivated feeding behavior. [1] Having only been recently discovered in 1999, ghrelin’s many putative functions have sparked interest in a variety of clinical applications. This brief review focuses on the significant effects of ghrelin.

agent1_snippets.title: Physical Activity and Weight Loss Maintenance -- Clinical Significance -- Appetite Hormone Regulation and Pharmacotherapy
agent1_snippets.content: Persistent hormonal adaptations following weight loss include decreased leptin and increased ghrelin levels, which heighten hunger and reduce satiety. [23] These hormonal changes can undermine efforts to maintain a reduced weight. GLP-1 receptor agonists have shown promise in mitigating these effects. A 2024 randomized trial demonstrated that combining GLP-1 receptor agonists with structured exercise resulted in approximately 6 kg greater weight loss, which was maintained after medication cessation, compared with GLP-1 therapy alone.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Obesity Neurohormonal Appetite And Satiety Control -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Ghrelin and leptin exist as key hormones with regulatory effects of clinical significance in treating various disorders. In cancer cachexia, ghrelin has already shown promise as a therapeutic option with its anti-inflammatory action on cancer cells. Through its effects on muscle catabolism, anti-apoptotic mechanism, and reducing the adverse effects of chemotherapy, ghrelin may help treat cancer cachexia. [35] The effects of ghrelin on the rest of the body's metabolism are vast, and avenues for anti-inflammation, improvement of cardiac performance, and stress modulation are continually under investigation. [36] Synthetic ghrelin-receptor agonist analogs like Anamorelin have shown beneficial effects. [37] The effects and circulation of leptin have also been experimented with regarding weight loss. [38] Many of these studies are still undergoing clinical trials, hoping they will have significant clinical benefits in the future.

agent1_snippets.title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Function -- Tirzepatide
agent1_snippets.content: Tirzepatide (brand name Zepbound) represents the most significant advancement in obesity pharmacotherapy, as it is the first dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist approved for chronic weight management (see Table 1 ). [6] This innovative mechanism targets 2 complementary incretin pathways, resulting in unprecedented weight loss efficacy that surpasses that of all other obesity medications. In the SURMOUNT-1 trial, tirzepatide achieved a dose-dependent weight loss of 15.0%, 19.5%, and 20.9% with doses of 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively. [6]

agent1_snippets.content: Setmelanotide (IMCIVREE)  represents precision medicine in obesity treatment, specifically targeting rare genetic forms of obesity through melanocortin-4 receptor agonism (see Table 7 ). agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Gastric Inhibitory Peptide -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: The incretin effect is defined as the enhanced insulin secretion observed in response to oral glucose compared to intravenous glucose, despite equivalent plasma glucose levels. [3] GIP is one of the principal incretin hormones and, together with glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1), accounts for 25% to 70% of the postprandial insulin response. [4] For decades, GIP was regarded as an ineffective insulinotropic agent with limited therapeutic potential. The recent success of tirzepatide and emerging triple agonists targeting GLP-1, GIP, and glucagon receptors has renewed interest in GIP physiology and pharmacology.

agent1_snippets.title: Setmelanotide -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Setmelanotide is the first available anorexigenic agent, a melanocortin-4 receptor (MC4R) agonist for treating obesity caused by a rare genetic disorder. [1] Setmelanotide was approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) on November 25, 2020, for the indication of chronic weight management in adults and pediatrics six years of age and older due to the deficiency of proopiomelanocortin (POMC), proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 1 (PCSK1), or leptin receptor (LEPR) deficiency. [1] [2]

agent2_snippets.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise several agents, including exenatid, liraglutide, and lixisenatide. These GLP‑1 analogues are often combined with basal insulins to enhance glycaemic control in patients with diabetes. The fixed‑ratio combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide incorporates liraglutide, while the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide contains lixisenatide. By pairing GLP‑1 receptor agonists such as liraglutide or lixisenatide with long‑acting insulins, clinicians can exploit complementary mechanisms—insulin’s basal glucose‑lowering effect and GLP‑1 analogues’ glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, delayed gastric emptying, and appetite suppression—to achieve more effective and convenient diabetes management.

agent2_snippets.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues are a class of incretin‑based medications that include both exenatid and lixisenatide. Exenatid, as a member of the GLP‑1 analogue family, acts on the GLP‑1 receptor to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, and slow gastric emptying. Lixisenatide similarly belongs to the GLP‑1 analogue class and shares these pharmacologic effects. In addition to being an individual GLP‑1 analogue, lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin glargine/lixisenatide, which pairs basal insulin glargine with the GLP‑1 receptor agonist to provide combined basal insulin and incretin therapy for improved glycemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes. This integration underscores the therapeutic strategy of using GLP‑1 analogues both as standalone agents and as components of combination regimens to optimize blood glucose management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
agent2_snippets.content: Hormone antagonists (antagonisté hormonů) represent a pharmacologic class that includes specific agents such as mifepristone and atosiban. Mifepristone is an example of a hormone antagonist that blocks progesterone receptors and is used in medical termination of pregnancy and other therapeutic contexts. Atosiban, described through an inverse‑is‑a relationship, is also a hormone antagonist; it acts as an oxytocin receptor blocker to inhibit uterine contractions and is employed in the management of preterm labor. Recognizing both mifepristone and atosiban as members of the hormone antagonist class highlights their shared mechanism of opposing endogenous hormonal signaling while underscoring their distinct clinical applications.

agent2_snippets.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products and peripherally acting antiobesity products are both classified as antiobesity preparations, excl. diet products. The centrally acting antiobesity products category includes the agents setmelanotide and sibutramine, which achieve weight loss through mechanisms that act within the central nervous system. Peripherally acting antiobesity products represent a separate class that exerts its effects outside the central nervous system, although specific members are not listed here. Understanding the distinction between central and peripheral mechanisms of antiobesity preparations helps clinicians choose appropriate pharmacologic strategies for obesity management.

agent2_snippets.content: Somatropin and somatropin agonists constitute a therapeutic class of growth‑hormone–related agents, and this class includes the specific drugs mecasermin, sermorelin, somapacitan, and tesamorelin. Each of these agents functions as an agonist of the somatropin (human growth hormone) pathway, sharing a common mechanism of stimulating growth‑hormone receptors to promote anabolic and metabolic effects. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
agent2_snippets.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products constitute a pharmacologic class that promotes weight loss by influencing central nervous system pathways involved in appetite and energy balance. This class includes the combination therapy bupropion / naltrexone, the melanocortin‑4 receptor agonist setmelanotide, and the serotonin‑norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor sibutramine. Bupropion / naltrexone works by modulating reward and satiety circuits, setmelanotide targets genetic forms of obesity through melanocortin signaling, and sibutramine reduces appetite by increasing synaptic concentrations of serotonin and norepinephrine. Together, these centrally acting agents illustrate the diverse mechanisms by which central pathways can be leveraged to treat obesity, highlighting the importance of individualized therapeutic selection based on efficacy, safety profile, and underlying patient characteristics.

agent2_snippets.content: Hormony (hormones) constitute a broad class of biologically active substances, and within this class the subgroup progestiny (progestins) is recognized as a specific type of hormone. Glukagon is classified as a hormone, indicating that it belongs to the same overall family of endocrine regulators. In clinical practice, the use of glukagon is contraindicated with the drug ورم جزيري, meaning that this medication should be avoided when glucagon therapy is considered. Megestrolacetát (megestrol acetate) is identified as a member of the progestiny subgroup, confirming that it is a progestin and therefore part of the hormone class. Understanding these hierarchical relationships—hormones encompassing progestins, glucagon as a hormone, and megestrol acetate as a progestin—helps clinicians recognize the pharmacological context and avoid contraindicated drug combinations such as glucagon with ورم جزيري.
